content.text: An Azure datacenter is a physical building housing the servers, networking equipment, and infrastructure that powers Azure services. Understanding what a datacenter contains — and how Microsoft designs, connects, and secures them — helps you reason about reliability, latency, and compliance guarantees in your architecture.

Microsoft operates over 300 physical datacenters globally, organized into regions and Availability Zones. Each datacenter is designed for independent fault isolation: its own power feeds, cooling plant, and network path. A region typically groups multiple datacenters within a metropolitan area, linked by private fiber Microsoft owns end-to-end.

attrs.alt: Azure datacenter anatomy: Availability Zones containing multiple physically separate datacenters connected by private fiber

content.text: Each datacenter is divided into three functional layers: compute infrastructure, cooling and power, and network fabric. These operate independently enough that a failure in one layer does not cascade across the full datacenter.

Servers are grouped in racks, typically 40 to 48 units tall. Racks are arranged in rows separated by hot aisles (where hot exhaust exits servers) and cold aisles (where cooled air enters). Microsoft designs its own servers and operates them at a scale that lets it standardize every hardware component — custom CPUs, NICs, power supplies — which eliminates supply-chain dependencies on standard OEM parts.
Microsoft's Project Olympus and subsequent generations define open-spec hardware optimized for Azure workloads, including liquid-cooled compute for high-density GPU clusters.
each physical server runs a custom hypervisor (Azure Hypervisor) that isolates customer VMs from each other and from the host OS, with hardware root-of-trust attestation on every boot.

every server includes a hardware TPM 2.0 chip and a Microsoft-designed security chip (Pluton or its predecessors) for host attestation. Servers that fail attestation are quarantined before workloads are placed.
Azure datacenters run on redundant utility power feeds from at least two independent electrical substations wherever regulation and geography allow. Each feed goes through:
High-voltage transformers stepping utility voltage down to usable levels
Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS) on each rack row, providing 10 to 15 minutes of battery backup
Multiple diesel generators per datacenter building, each capable of running the full facility load
Automatic transfer switches (ATS) that switch to generators within milliseconds of utility power loss

Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) targets of 1.12 or below in newer facilities — meaning for every watt consumed by IT equipment, only 0.12 additional watts are used for cooling, lighting, and other overhead. Industry average PUE is typically 1.5 or higher.
Cooling is typically the largest non-IT energy consumer in a datacenter. Azure uses several cooling models depending on the facility's climate and hardware density:
cold air is directed to server intakes (cold aisle); hot exhaust is captured and routed back to cooling units without mixing, improving efficiency.
many Azure facilities in temperate climates use evaporative cooling instead of mechanical chillers for the majority of the year, significantly reducing power consumption.
high-density compute (especially GPU clusters) uses direct liquid cooling on processors, enabling much higher power densities than air cooling permits.
Microsoft's Natick project explored underwater datacenters in cold seawater for natural cooling; while not in production at scale, the research informed current efficiency targets.
Network Fabric: Connecting Datacenters to the World

The network inside and between Azure datacenters is a custom, software-defined fabric that Microsoft owns and operates. There is no shared public internet on the data path between Azure services — all inter-datacenter traffic travels over Microsoft's private WAN.
Connects 40-48 servers in a rack at 100 Gbps per server
Connect all ToR switches in a pod; provide east-west fabric
Connect DC to Azure's global backbone
Customer-to-Azure traffic entry via public internet or ExpressRoute

SmartNICs (FPGAs and custom silicon) are deployed in every server. They offload network processing — packet forwarding, encryption, QoS — from the CPU, freeing CPU cycles for customer workloads and reducing latency by processing packets in hardware rather than the host OS network stack.
Azure datacenters operate under a multi-layer physical security model. Microsoft publishes the layers but not specific site details:
Perimeter security: vehicle barriers, security fencing, motion-sensing cameras, 24x7 on-site security personnel
Building entry: badge-only access, mantrap airlocks on all entry points, no tailgating policy enforced electronically
Data hall access: biometric or multi-factor badge + PIN access to computing floors; visitor access logged and escorted
Rack-level: individual locked rack enclosures; keys managed through hardware key management systems
Decommissioning: storage devices are degaussed and physically destroyed to NIST 800-88 standards before leaving the facility
Microsoft's datacenters are ISO 27001, SOC 1, SOC 2, and SOC 3 certified, among other standards. The physical security controls are audited as part of these certifications.
Azure has committed to being carbon-negative by 2030 and removing all historical carbon emissions by 2050. Datacenter infrastructure choices are central to this commitment:
Microsoft purchases renewable energy credits (RECs) and power purchase agreements (PPAs) to match 100% of its global electricity consumption with renewable sources, achieved in 2025.

cooling accounts for much of datacenter water consumption. Microsoft targets returning more water to watersheds than it consumes in operations, through water-efficient cooling and watershed replenishment programs.
decommissioned servers are refurbished and reused wherever possible. Only hardware that cannot be reused is destroyed and recycled.
Microsoft targets zero-waste certification (90% landfill diversion) for all owned and operated datacenter facilities.
What You Can and Cannot Know About Datacenters

Microsoft does not publish the exact locations of individual datacenters, the number of servers per region, or the physical capacity of any specific facility. This is deliberate security policy: an adversary who knows a precise datacenter address could target it physically or use it to plan regional attacks.
Region locations (approximate metropolitan area, e.g. 'East US' = Virginia)
Whether a region has Availability Zones and how many (always 3 when present)
Compliance certifications earned by the datacenters in each region
Sustainability metrics in the annual Sustainability Report
Service availability per region (which Azure services are available in which region)

From an application architecture perspective, what matters is not which specific building your workload runs in, but that the guarantees hold: the SLA is met, zone isolation is real, and compliance certifications are current.

# List all Azure regions with their geographical display names
az account list-locations \
  --query "[*].{region:name, display:displayName, lat:metadata.latitude, lon:metadata.longitude}" \
  --output table

# Check which services are available in a specific region
az provider list \
  --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].namespace" \
  --output tsv | head -20

# Check whether a specific region has Availability Zones
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?name=='eastus'].{name:name, zones:availabilityZoneMappings[*].physicalZone}" \
  --output json

Understanding the scale difference between an Azure datacenter and a typical enterprise datacenter helps explain why economies of scale exist and why certain guarantees (like 99.99% SLA) are achievable in Azure but not in most on-premises deployments.
Multiple independent utility feeds + N+1 or 2N generators
Evaporative or liquid cooling at facility scale
400 Gbps+ per rack; Tbps inter-region WAN
Small IT team, shared with business
Custom silicon, 3 to 5 year cycles, continuous refresh
ISO 27001, SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, FedRAMP where applicable
How Datacenters Map to Azure Resource Concepts

When you work with Azure resources through the portal, CLI, or IaC, you interact with abstractions that map to physical datacenter infrastructure. Understanding these mappings helps you reason about what guarantees hold and where your data actually lives.
Logical container only — no physical location
Shared lifecycle; no physical isolation guarantee
Data residency within a geography; ≥2ms latency between AZs
1 or more physically separate datacenters in the region
Independent power, cooling, network from other AZs
Specific physical server(s) in a datacenter
Isolation at physical host level; no neighbor tenants on that server
Cluster of racks in the same datacenter
Synchronous writes to all 3 AZs; survives one AZ failure
Private fiber from customer location to Azure datacenter edge
Private, dedicated bandwidth bypassing the public internet
Proximity Placement Groups: Pinning to a Datacenter

A Proximity Placement Group (PPG) is the closest Azure concept to 'put this resource in the same datacenter building as that one.' It ensures low-latency co-location by placing VMs in the same physical rack cluster. Use PPGs for latency-sensitive workloads like HPC clusters, SAP HANA scale-out, or high-frequency trading applications.

# Create a Proximity Placement Group
az ppg create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPPG \
  --location eastus \
  --type Standard

# Create a VM inside the PPG (collocated in the same physical cluster)
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-HPC-1 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_HB120rs_v3 \
  --proximity-placement-group myPPG \
  --zone 1

# Add a second VM to the same PPG
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-HPC-2 \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_HB120rs_v3 \
  --proximity-placement-group myPPG \
  --zone 1

# Show PPG intent and contained resources
az ppg show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myPPG \
  --query "{type:proximityPlacementGroupType, vms:virtualMachines[*].id}" \
  --output json

PPGs trade zone resilience for latency: all VMs in a PPG are in the same physical cluster, meaning a single rack-cluster failure could take them all down. Only use PPGs when sub-millisecond latency between VMs is a hard requirement.

Azure Dedicated Hosts give you an entire physical server allocated exclusively to your workloads. No other customer's VMs run on that host. This enables physical isolation for compliance requirements (some regulations prohibit shared hardware), predicable performance (no noisy neighbor on the CPU or NUMA bus), and control over the host's maintenance schedule.

# Create a Dedicated Host Group (a collection of physical hosts in an AZ)
az vm host group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myHostGroup \
  --location eastus \
  --zone 1 \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 2

# Allocate a dedicated host in the group
az vm host create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --host-group myHostGroup \
  --name myDedicatedHost \
  --sku DSv3-Type3 \
  --platform-fault-domain 0

# Deploy a VM onto the dedicated host
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myIsolatedVM \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --host-group myHostGroup \
  --zone 1

# List available dedicated host SKUs in a region
az vm host list-resize-options \
  --location eastus \
  --output table

Dedicated Hosts are priced per host per hour, regardless of how many VMs run on them. A host with capacity for 36 D-series VMs costs the same whether 1 or 36 VMs are running. Azure Hybrid Benefit applies to VMs on dedicated hosts — bring your existing Windows Server or RHEL/SLES licenses to reduce costs.
Physical: only your VMs on the host
Per host per hour (fixed regardless of VMs on it)
Full NUMA bus and L3 cache exclusive to you
You set platform-fault-domain at host group level

Azure must occasionally update host firmware, apply hardware microcode patches, or retire aging hardware. These events can impact VMs running on affected hosts. Azure's maintenance strategy has three modes:
the host pauses VMs for 30 seconds or less while applying a live update. No reboot required. Most platform updates fall into this category.

the host reboots after updates. Azure schedules this in maintenance windows published 35 days in advance via Service Health. VMs with redeployment not blocked can be migrated to another host before the window.
failing or end-of-life hardware triggers a live migration of VMs to a new host. Azure attempts live migration transparently; if that is not possible it requests a scheduled reboot.

# Check scheduled maintenance events for VMs in a resource group
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-07-01" \
  --query "value[*].{name:name, maintenance:properties.instanceView.maintenanceRedeployStatus}" \
  --output json

# Subscribe to Azure Service Health alerts for planned maintenance
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "PlannedMaintenanceAlert" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --condition "category=PlannedMaintenance" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG

# Redeploy a VM before a maintenance window (moves it to a different host)
az vm redeploy \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM

Azure datacenters carry a large portfolio of third-party audit certifications. These certifications are renewed annually (or more frequently for some standards) and cover the physical facility controls, operational procedures, and logical controls together.
Baseline security controls audited by third party annually
Required for customers with financial reporting obligations
Security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, privacy
Most broadly required for enterprise cloud workloads
Can be published to customers without NDA
Required for cardholder data environment workloads
Required for federal agency workloads in Azure Government
Confirms DR processes for the datacenter are tested and documented
Microsoft signs a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) for covered services

# List Azure compliance offerings programmatically via the Compliance REST API
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Security/assessmentMetadata?api-version=2021-06-01" \
  --query "value[?contains(properties.displayName, 'ISO')].{name:properties.displayName}" \
  --output table

# Get compliance state for a subscription (Azure Policy)
az policy state list \
  --query "[?complianceState=='NonCompliant'].{resource:resourceId, policy:policyDefinitionName}" \
  --output table | head -20
Resiliency Design Principles from Datacenter Architecture
The physical design of Azure datacenters directly informs the resiliency patterns you should apply at the application layer. Each physical boundary corresponds to an Azure construct you can leverage:
Single rack power strip or top-of-rack switch
Spread VMs across ≥2 fault domains in an Availability Set
Fire, flood, power failure to a building
Deploy across all 3 AZs; use zone-redundant managed services
Use region pairs and GRS/GZRS for critical data

The principle is: match your redundancy scope to the blast radius of the failure you want to survive. Rack-level failures are common; zone-level failures are rare; region-level failures are very rare. Cost increases with redundancy scope, so choose the appropriate level for each service tier.

A service with 99.9% availability can experience 8.7 hours of downtime per year. Zone-redundant deployments (99.99%) reduce that to 52 minutes. Multi-region active-active can push below 5 minutes. Choose the right level for your SLA target, not the maximum because it feels safer.
ExpressRoute: Private Connectivity to Azure Datacenters

ExpressRoute provides a private, dedicated network connection from your on-premises environment directly to Azure datacenter edge routers, bypassing the public internet entirely. Traffic flows over MPLS circuits provisioned by network providers (AT&T, Equinix, Megaport, and others) who peer with Microsoft at ExpressRoute exchange locations.

From the datacenter infrastructure perspective, ExpressRoute terminates at an Azure datacenter's edge meet-me room where Microsoft routers peer with the provider's equipment. Once on the Microsoft network, your traffic travels over the same private WAN backbone that connects Azure datacenters to each other.

# Create an ExpressRoute circuit (prerequisites: provider circuit already ordered)
az network express-route create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myExpressRouteCircuit \
  --location eastus \
  --bandwidth 1000 \
  --peering-location "Washington DC" \
  --provider "Equinix" \
  --sku-tier Standard \
  --sku-family MeteredData

# Link an ExpressRoute circuit to a VNet gateway
az network vpn-connection create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myERConnection \
  --vnet-gateway1 myERGateway \
  --express-route-circuit2 /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Network/expressRouteCircuits/myExpressRouteCircuit \
  --routing-weight 0

# Check ExpressRoute circuit status
az network express-route show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myExpressRouteCircuit \
  --query "{state:serviceProviderProvisioningState, bandwidth:serviceProviderProperties.bandwidthInMbps}"

# List ExpressRoute peering locations
az network express-route peering-location list \
  --provider Equinix \
  --query "[*].{location:name, city:address}" \
  --output table

ExpressRoute Global Reach lets you connect two on-premises networks through the Microsoft backbone, using Azure datacenters as relay points — useful for connecting branch offices internationally without building private WAN links between them.
Azure Datacenters and the Shared Responsibility Model

The Shared Responsibility Model defines what Microsoft is responsible for and what you are responsible for when running workloads in Azure. Datacenter infrastructure sits entirely on Microsoft's side of the line.
VNet design, NSG rules, firewall policies
None for PaaS; VM patching for IaaS
None — Microsoft manages; customers cannot access
Patching VM guest OS (for IaaS VMs)

The datacenter's physical controls — power, cooling, physical access, hardware lifecycle — are under Microsoft's exclusive control. Customers cannot inspect, access, or influence these directly. Third-party audits (SOC 2, ISO 27001) are the mechanism by which customers receive assurance that these controls are working correctly.

While you cannot inspect individual datacenters, Azure exposes datacenter health through Service Health and Azure Monitor. Service Health reports outages, planned maintenance, and advisory notices scoped to the regions and services you use.

# Check current service health issues in a region
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/events?api-version=2022-10-01" \
  --query "value[?properties.impactedServices[?impactedRegions[?regionName=='East US']]].{title:properties.title, status:properties.status, type:properties.eventType}" \
  --output json

# Set up a Service Health alert for all events in East US
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "ServiceHealthAllEastUS" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --condition "category=ServiceHealth" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG

# Check the health of a specific resource (e.g., a VM)
az rest \
  --method get \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/availabilityStatuses/current?api-version=2020-01-01" \
  --query "properties.{status:availabilityState, reason:reasonType, summary:summary}" \
  --output json
go to Azure Portal → Service Health to see a map of active incidents, planned maintenance windows, and health advisories for your subscriptions.
Service Health stores 90 days of past incidents. Use this to understand how frequently your region has experienced issues and for post-incident analysis.
per-resource health status (Available, Degraded, Unavailable, Unknown) available via REST or portal for VMs, storage accounts, SQL, and other resources.
create alerts on ServiceHealth category events to receive email/SMS/webhook notifications when an issue is detected in your region.

Azure datacenters are custom-designed facilities with independent power, cooling, and network infrastructure. Multiple datacenters are grouped into Availability Zones, and multiple AZs form a region. The physical isolation between AZs — separate buildings, power feeds, and network paths — is the foundation for the zone-redundancy guarantees that Azure services provide.
Each datacenter has independent power (multi-feed utility + UPS + generators), cooling, and network, enabling single-facility fault isolation.
Microsoft owns the network between datacenters within a region; inter-datacenter traffic never traverses the public internet.
Physical security follows a layered model audited under ISO 27001, SOC 2, and other standards.
Microsoft does not publish individual datacenter addresses; region-level location (metropolitan area) is the finest grain publicly available.
At scale, Azure's operational model delivers reliability and sustainability metrics that are impractical for typical enterprise datacenters to match.
